item.title: On Slowness
item.description:
There is a road I used to take that added twelve minutes to my commute. It wound past a field that changed with every season — stubble in autumn, a brilliant sheet of green in spring, and in summer a kind of gold so pure it seemed borrowed from somewhere else.

I stopped taking it somewhere around the third year. The twelve minutes began to feel like theft. I had things to do, messages to answer, a future to arrive at.

item.title: March Rain
item.description:
Woke to the sound of it before I opened my eyes. Rain on the skylight — that specific rhythm that means the day will be slow and permitted.

Made coffee and stood at the window for longer than I meant to. The street was nearly empty. A man walked by with no umbrella, unhurried, face tilted up. I felt oddly moved by this.

There is something I want to say about threshold weather — the kind that lives between seasons, neither one thing nor another. I am always most myself at these edges.

item.title: Threshold
item.description:
Between the lamp and the window
there is a country I return to —

not sleep, not waking,
but that particular amber

where the day
unstitches itself from me

and I become
briefly, briefly

nobody’s.

item.title: Field Notes
item.description:
Field Notes began as a small PDF sent to forty-three friends. It grew, the way things do when they are made without expectations — slowly, and then all at once.

The journal publishes writers who pay attention to place: essays, dispatches, photographs made with intention. Each issue is organized around a season and a question.

Working on Field Notes taught me that editing is also a kind of writing — a listening for what wants to be said, and a clearing of everything that isn’t that.
